params.text:
The principles of good record keeping

Whatever you are writing, the same core principles apply. A good record is:

  • Factual and accurate — only what you know to be true.
  • Legible — clear enough for anyone to read and understand.
  • Contemporaneous and timely — written at the time or as soon as possible afterwards, not days later from memory.
  • Signed and dated — with the time too, so it is clear who wrote it and when.
  • Objective — facts, not personal opinions or assumptions.
  • Free of unnecessary jargon or abbreviations that others might misread.
  • Person-centred and respectful — written in language you would be happy for the person and their family to read.

params.alt: A care worker writing notes on a clipboard during a visit

params.taskDescription: Complete this summary of good record keeping by dragging each word into the gap it belongs in.
params.textField: A good record must be *factual* and accurate, easy to read and *legible*, and written *promptly* while events are fresh. Every entry should be *signed* and dated by its author. Records must stay *objective*, describing facts rather than opinions, and be written in *respectful*, person-centred language.
